---
title: "| Data Science With an Eye Towards Sustainability    \n| P7: Project Check-In and Research Questions Update\n"
author: "Quinn & Peter"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    split_by: none
    toc: no
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidycensus)
library(sf)
library(DT)
library(ggplot2)
library(janitor)
library(GGally)
library(RColorBrewer)  # for color palettes
library(sf)            # for working with spatial data
library(leaflet)       # for highly customizable mapping
library(ggthemes)      # for more themes (including theme_map())
library(socviz)        # for the US county map
library(usmap)         # for plot_usmap
library(usdata)       # for abbr2state function
library(choroplethr)   # another option to make choropleths
library(choroplethrMaps)
library(maps)
library(rvest)
library(plotly)

```

It is time for another project checkpoint. By now, your research questions should be significantly narrowed, and you should be well on your way to developing some visualizations to answer at least some of the research questions. **Each group should hand in one html document for the following exercises.**

```{r, include=FALSE}
#imports Data
mass_solar_messy <- read_csv("~/Documents/GitHub/solar-incentive-adoption/Solar-Project/Solar-PV-Systems-in-MA-as-of-Jan-2025-readable.csv", 
    col_types = cols(`Date In Service` = col_date(format = "%m/%d/%Y"), 
        `Total Cost with Design Fees` = col_number(), 
        `Total Grant` = col_number()))

#cleans names

mass_solar_clean<-mass_solar_messy |>
  janitor::clean_names()|>
   mutate(year=year(date_in_service), month=month(date_in_service),weekday=wday(date_in_service), day=day(date_in_service))

#################

#pulling from sunroof file
project_sunroof_census_tract <- read_csv("~/Documents/GitHub/solar-incentive-adoption/Solar-Project/project-sunroof-city.csv", 
    col_types = cols(state_name = col_factor(levels = c("Alabama", 
        "Alaska", "Arizona", "Arkansas", 
        "California", "Colorado", "Connecticut", 
        "Delaware", "Florida", "Georgia", 
        "Hawaii", "Idaho", "Illinois", "Indiana", 
        "Iowa", "Kansas", "Kentucky", "Louisiana", 
        "Maine", "Maryland", "Massachusetts", 
        "Michigan", "Minnesota", "Mississippi", 
        "Missouri", "Montana", "Nebraska", 
        "Nevada", "New Hampshire", "New Jersey", 
        "New Mexico", "New York", "North Carolina", 
        "North Dakota", "Ohio", "Oklahoma", 
        "Oregon", "Pennsylvania", "Rhode Island", 
        "South Carolina", "South Dakota", 
        "Tennessee", "Texas", "Utah", "Vermont", 
        "Virginia", "Washington", "West Virginia", 
        "Wisconsin", "Wyoming"))))
wrangled_sunroof <- project_sunroof_census_tract |>
  filter(state_name == "Massachusetts") |>
  drop_na(count_qualified)

#scraping city/town data from wikipedia - population
url<-"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Massachusetts"
webpage<-rvest::read_html(url)
city <- webpage |>
  html_nodes(".static-row-numbers td:nth-child(1)") |>
  html_text2() 
county <- webpage |>
  html_nodes(".static-row-numbers td:nth-child(3)") |>
  html_text2() 
type <- webpage |>
  html_nodes(".static-row-numbers td:nth-child(2)") |>
  html_text2() 
pop <- webpage |>
  html_nodes(".static-row-numbers td:nth-child(5)") |>
  html_text2() 
mass_city <- tibble(county=county,city=city, pop_2020=pop, type=type)
mass_city <- mass_city|>
 # grepl("seat", county)
  mutate(county=sub("(seat)"," ", county))|>
  mutate(county=sub("\\("," ", county))|>
  mutate(county=sub("\\)"," ", county))

#scraping city/town median income data from wikipedia
url<-"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Massachusetts_locations_by_per_capita_income"
webpage<-rvest::read_html(url)

town <- webpage |>
  html_nodes("td:nth-child(2)") |>
  html_text2() 
town <- tibble(town=town) 
town2 <- town[16:356,c(1)]

median_income <- webpage |>
  html_nodes("td:nth-child(5)") |>
  html_text2() 
median_income <- tibble(median_income=median_income)
median_income2 <- median_income[16:356,c(1)]

median_income_town <- town2 |>
  bind_cols(town2, median_income2) |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(town=town_1, median_income_per_capita=as.numeric(gsub("[$, ]", "" , median_income)))|>
  select(town, median_income_per_capita)

#joining data frames
sunroof_towns <- left_join(wrangled_sunroof, median_income_town, by = c("region_name"="town")) |> 
  select(region_name, count_qualified, percent_covered, percent_qualified, number_of_panels_median, number_of_panels_total, existing_installs_count, median_income_per_capita) |>
  drop_na()
```

\

# Names & Workflow

*List all group members. Discuss a plan for completing this assignment. How will you put together your shared Rmd? How will you divide the work? When will you meet to work on the assignment? Write this down in your Rmd.*

*Also, if you are using one, write down your GitHub repository.*

We will continue using our GitHub repo and the GitHub Desktop app:\
<https://github.com/QuinnVerrill/solar-incentive-adoption>

# Team Health and Progress

*Please take a few minutes to reflect collectively on how things have been going so far in terms of working together as a team. What has worked well? What hasn't? Are there work flow changes you want to make for the remainder of the project?*

\
Well, we got off to a rocky start. But, now the rock is rolling down the hill and we're along for the ride... things are great! … until the rock hits the bottom, hopefully at which point we will be done with the project!

In all seriousness, the project is going well. We both feel like we have solid direction and story to tell with the data we've found. In terms of workflow, we’ve scheduled weekly check-in, which has been helpful for touching base on progress before we have work time in class. 

Overall, our work on this project has been pretty chill and needs to ramp up a little bit.

# Refined Research Questions

*Please specify your updated research question(s) here. At this point, I am expecting 1-4 research questions that are more closely connected.*

1: How have state and Federal incentives impacted the adoption of residential rooftop solar in Massachusetts over time?

-   How does this vary by municipality?

-   What has the timeline of state/federal incentives been for solar?

2: How is solar adoption and type of solar facility linked to socioeconomic factors across county/townships in Massachusetts?

-   How does this vary by income per capita of each municipality?

# Connections to Sustainability

*How are your research questions connected to sustainability? What are your personal learning goals related to sustainability for this project? You may wish to refer back to the [UN Sustainable Development Goals](https://sdgs.un.org/goals), the [Inner Development Goals](https://innerdevelopmentgoals.org/), and the [Engineering for One Planet](https://engineeringforoneplanet.org/wp-content/uploads/EOP_Framework_2023.pdf?), [Six Pillars of Climate Justice](https://centerclimatejustice.universityofcalifornia.edu/what-is-climate-justice/), and [Just Transition Principles](https://climatejusticealliance.org/just-transition/) frameworks. As a team, what sustainability background, analysis, and storytellling do you want to include in the scope of your project and in your final artifacts?\
*\
So far, there have been interesting trends in terms of adoption rates, and their connection to larger political events. The graph below shows how residential adoption in Massachusetts peaked around 2016, and has generally been declining since then. When thinking about renewable energy, this is an interesting trend from a sustainability perspective.

We hope to tell a story about solar siting in the built environment as an ideal implementation of solar. Then we want to talk about how incentives can influence these trends. 

```{r}
total_solar<-mass_solar_clean|>
  mutate(has_grant= total_grant>0)|>
  group_by(year)|>
  summarise(count=n(), grant=sum(has_grant, na.rm=TRUE))

total_solar

p <- ggplot(total_solar, aes(x=year))+
  geom_col(aes(y=count))+
  geom_col(aes(y=grant), fill="green")+
  ggtitle("Mass total solar instalations over time, with grant recieved ")+
labs(y="Number of Instalations", x="Year")
  

ggplotly(p)
```

\

# Data

*Identify the data sets you are using to explore the research questions above. These data sets may already be in csv form, you may acquire them (e.g., scraped with rvest, via public API, or from an SQL database), or you may plan on collecting some extra data. For each data set, summarize the following:*

-   *data source*
-   *data description - what's being measured?*
-   *data limitations (eg: are the data recent? do they contain all variables you might want?)*
-   *data dimensions - how much data do you have?*
-   *how might the data be joined with other data you have?*

We have used the following data sources:

-   Project Sunroof: 

    -   Provides data broken down by town about the number of current solar installations, how eligible/qualified the houses in the area are based on yearly sun exposure metrics, and the kinds of incentives currently available in an area.

-   Median income per capita (Wikipedia)

    -   Scraped from this website (<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Massachusetts_locations_by_per_capita_income>) because the tidycensus package can’t break down information by town/city, only by “place”, tract, or county.

-   MassCEC PTS (production tracking system) <https://www.masscec.com/production-tracking-system-pts> 

    -   Provides data about individual solar projects, grants/incentives received, going back to 2003

# Research Question 1

*Restate research question 1. Construct 3-4 relevant visualizations that provide insight on / help answer this question and piece these together to tell a short story. For each visualization, 1-2 sentences will suffice.*

1: How have state and Federal incentives impacted the adoption of residential rooftop solar in Massachusetts over time?

-   How does this vary by municipality?

![](data:image/png;base64,)

This is a plot that shows the yearly solar installation trend, faceted by municipality, and along with the number of those installations which received grant funding.

-   What has the timeline of state/federal incentives been for solar?

![](data:image/png;base64,)

This is a graph of year and month with the number of solar installations, our hope with this plot is to add annotations which illustrate timelines with state and federal tax incentives. \

# Research Question 2 (and possibly 3 and 4)

*Repeat the previous exercise for your second and possibly third or fourth research questions. Make a new section for each question.*

2: How is solar adoption and type of solar facility linked to socioeconomic factors across county/townships in Massachusetts?

-   How does this vary by income per capita of each municipality?

```{r}
ggplot(sunroof_towns, aes(x=median_income_per_capita,y=percent_qualified)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=FALSE)+
  ggtitle("Median Income per Capita vs Percent Qualified for Rooftop Solar", subtitle="Broken down by town/city in MA" )+
  labs(x="Median Income per Capita ($)", y="Percent Qualified")
```

This plot might tell an interesting story based on the clusters of data points. Lower median income seems to be slightly correlated to more houses in a town/city being qualified for residential rooftop solar (qualification based on Sunroof criteria of total yearly sun exposure in Kw). This is an interesting finding in combination with the following spatial visualization of median income per capital in MA:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

In this plot, many tracts with stand-out higher median yearly income are in the city’s outer ring. In the previous plot, there seems to be a small cluster of towns/cities that have a lower percentage of qualification but higher median income. 

```{r}
ggplot(sunroof_towns, aes(x=median_income_per_capita,y=number_of_panels_median)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=FALSE) +
  ggtitle("Median Income per Capita vs Median Number of Installed Rooftop Solar Panels", subtitle="Broken down by town/city in MA")+
  labs(x="Median Income per Capita ($)", y="Number of Panels Median")
```

Unfortunately, this plot doesn’t seem to have any useful information, beyond illustrating a few outlier towns/cities.

```{r}
ggplot(sunroof_towns, aes(x=median_income_per_capita,y=existing_installs_count)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=FALSE) +
  ggtitle("Median Income per Capita vs Existing Rooftop Solar Installations", subtitle="Broken down by town/city in MA")+
  labs(x="Median Income per Capita ($)", y="Existing Installs")
```

This plot also doesn’t seem to yield much useful information, but there do still seem to be some interesting outliers that would be interesting to examine further.

# Next Steps

*Identify next steps in your analysis. Do you need additional data? Do you need to do more cleaning or wrangling? Do you need to do some statistical modeling and inference? What additional visualizations would be helpful? How are the stories coming together?*

Quinn’s next steps are to continue working with the project sunroof data. This will include:

-   Identifying and researching the outlier towns, cross-referencing them with the Mass CEC PTS data to see the progression of their residential solar installations and grants over time 

    -   This will hopefully allow us to tell a very specific story about how a place has successfully incentivized residential rooftop solar adoption

-   Scraping lists of current incentives for each zip code in MA from the project sunroof website

Peter’s next steps are to refine some of the graphics to have keys, and to do some research on solar incentives at the state and federal level, with the goal of combining this information with the graphs to make labeled graphics to tell a story. I will also help with cross referencing the data for outliers. \

# Contributions

*Summarize each group member's specific contributions to this checkpoint assignment.*

Quinn mostly tackled the second research question, which involved wrangling Project Sunroof data for MA and combining it with census data in various ways. 

Peter worked on further data analysis for the first question and Mass CEC data set, and wrangled and made some visualizations
